article_meta.title: Politics Why Minnesotans Still Haven't Backed Down From Fighting ICE It's freezing

transcript.content:
You are a political lean classifier. Classify the article's political lean using the 5-class AllSides scale. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "lean": "Left"|"Lean Left"|"Center"|"Lean Right"|"Right",
  "confidence": 0.0 to 1.0,
  "reasoning": "string",
  "key_indicators": ["string"]
}
Scale:
  Left: strong consistent left-leaning editorial choices
  Lean Left: moderate left tilt, some balance present
  Center: balanced, neither side consistently favored
  Lean Right: moderate right tilt, some balance present
  Right: strong consistent right-leaning editorial choices
Confidence: 0.5-0.7 subtle lean, 0.8-0.95 clear lean, 0.95+ unambiguous.

Classify the political lean of this article:

---
HEADLINE: Politics Why Minnesotans Still Haven't Backed Down From Fighting ICE It's freezing
SOURCE: Slate

ARTICLE:

Politics Why Minnesotans Still Haven't Backed Down From Fighting ICE It's freezing. It's terrifying. And it's potential deadly. But anti-ICE activists are not giving up. By Laura Jedeed Feb 11, 20265:50 AM Roberto Schmidt/AFP via Getty Images Sign up for the Slatest to get the most insightful analysis, criticism, and advice out there, delivered to your inbox daily. "Sorry, I don't mean to interrupt you guys." The man crunches his way toward us, through this snow-covered field and past rows upon rows of identical wooden tombstones, then gestures back in the direction he came from. "I wanted to make sure you're aware that there's ICE right there." This is how it happens, I was told the day before. Immigration and Customs Enforcement moves fast in Minneapolis, the way you have to when an entire city is against you. Volunteers call in license plates to volunteers who check those plates against databases created by other volunteers. Once identified, these agents have minutes at best before residents with whistles and horns and furious loud voices gather to alert their neighbors to the threat in their midst. If you're more than five minutes away from a sighting, odds are you'll be too late. No, the best way to find ICE in Minneapolis is to stay in one place. Go about your day. Eventually, you'll see them. I am standing with two Minneapolis residents who go by Tim and Star, looking out at the Say Their Names memorial. The art installation consists of over 100 tombstones, each with identical black fists and names I remember from 2020. Philandro Castile. Ahmaud Arbery. And, of course, George Floyd, who took his final breath two blocks south of here. Minneapolis is no stranger to tragedy, or to the white-hot spotlight of international media attention, or to banding together in the face of a crisis. "In some ways, it primed the pump," Tim told me, referring to the George Floyd protests in the summer of 2020. Like everyone I spoke to, Tim and Star ascribe Minneapolis' successful mobilization to lessons learned during that time. "We have a lot of media coming in and asking, 'Oh, what's your organization? Who are you with?' And it's like, we don't have an organization. We're not with anyone," Star told me. Advertisement "We're just neighbors. That's how we know each other," Tim confirmed. "We live here." Star nodded. " One of the things that I'm not sure that people are realizing outside of Minneapolis: The headlines say that Alex Pretti was a protester," they say. "He wasn't at a protest. He wasn't at—" And then we turned to watch the man approaching us through the snow—the man now telling us ICE has pulled up by an apartment building across the field. "We've got the block, we're situated," he says. "I just want to make sure—in case anybody's targetable or something like that." None of us are targetable. Tim and Star want to help, and I want to see the action. Just as roadkill summons vultures, so too does human misery summon journalists, who travel across the country to shove cameras and recorders at people enduring things no one should experience. People in Minneapolis broadly understand that documentation is important, that the world needs to know what's happening, that journalists can help spread the word. But the line between journalism and pornography grows vanishingly thin at times like these. Newsworthy tales of ICE atrocities can easily become violent entertainment for a ravenous national audience; action-movie sequences carried out by caricatures, grief and trauma commodified for mass consumption. Star offers me a 3D-printed orange whistle and I open my mouth to say that as a journalist I can't participate—but the words won't come out. A sudden vision of myself in the future, talking to grand-nieces and -nephews: Yes, I had a chance to warn people about the secret police coming to abduct them, disappear them, or release them into this dangerous cold without a coat or cell phone, but journalistic ethics, you see … Hell with it. I take the whistle. At last, the cars arrive. The bearded man gives us a count: One, two, three … The noise is deafening: Horns blare, whistles pierce the piercing cold. "ICE OUT!" the residents chant, over and over again. "We are all Minneapolis!" someone shouts. "We are all Minnesota! We are all the motherfucking United States of America!" The world might breathe a sigh of relief, but Minneapolis can't afford it. ICE is here, and ICE has killed, and ICE may kill again. Here the city stands: residents with whistles standing against men with guns and flak vests. The ICE SUV stays put despite the unrelenting noise and a neighborhood now on high alert. I start shivering five minutes in; the man in sweatpants and Crocs appears unbothered. There are over 30 people here now; residents have heeded the call or arrived from rapid-response patrol. A white Toyota stops between the crowd and the SUV and a man with a mustache leans out the passenger window with a camera: no words, no eye contact, just the indifferent glassy eye of a zoom lens and fingers to focus it on people as they do something others have died doing. Someone has brought a megaphone; residents take turns leading chants. "My grandmother was a sharecropper in Arkansas, and I know that this is the same thing," a man shouts at one point as whistles shrill and a handheld siren wails. "The same fight that my people fought, the same fight that my grandma fought, is the same fight that we are fighting to protect our undocumented migrant neighbors!" ICE-watch reports more agents incoming, but the crowd has only grown and no one is going anywhere. Five minutes becomes 10, 10 becomes 15. "All right folks, we're going to sing 'We are not afraid!' " someone shouts into the megaphone, and so they do; not shouting or chanting but singing, in tune and with one voice: We are not afraid We are not afraid We are not afraid today Oh, deep in my heart I do believe That we are not afraid today And then, to the sound of sporadic laughter, the amplified voice sings a slightly altered version: ICE will leave ICE will leave Oh, deep in my heart I do believe ICE will leave RIGHT THE FUCK NOW! GET THE FUCK OUT OF MINNEAPOLIS! THIS IS OUR FUCKING STREET! Laughter and cheers and the handheld siren wails and the man with the megaphone keeps shouting: "The people are not with you! The people are with us! And they want you to get the fuck out of our city!" A few minutes later, the SUV lurches forward, then drives slowly down the street. The residents cheer and taunt and follow from a safe distance. Whatever the bastards had planned for the apartment complex will have to wait for another day. "That's the most exciting laundry I've ever done," a man with a gray beard says dryly, then walks back into everyday life, to the extent that everyday life is possible anymore. Why do people continue to push back, knowing what it might mean? It's the question I'm here to ask, and so I approach two people and ask it. " I'd love to know what inspires you to come out here, knowing that people have been killed doing this shit. You can also tell me to fuck off." The resident studies me for several seconds. "I'm not going to tell you to fuck off," she says at last. "But I don't think I want to answer." Advertisement "It feels like the only way through it is through it," Tim says when I ask him the same question a few minutes later. "Some of this is—it's happening to us. And it's a choice to stand up, but—" He pauses. "We don't have a lot of options." The day before this standoff on 37th and Columbus, I spent all day in a rapid-response chat with dozens of volunteers: people who had to excuse themselves to pick up kids from school, people on their way home willing to spare an hour to keep their community safe. The tone was matter-of-fact, direct, resolute, but moments of fear crept in. I could hear it in the voice of a woman who reported an ICE agent getting out of his car, and in two watchers who declined to follow an agent who drove "aggressively" toward them in his car. "Stay safe," they told each other by way of goodbye, with the kind of intensity reserved for people who understand that might not be possible. Later that night, I went to a "Hotel Wide-Awake" demonstration outside the four-star Hilton in downtown Minneapolis where ICE has rented a block of rooms. Hundreds of protesters stood outside in subzero temperatures and made as much noise as possible—banging pots, blowing whistles, drumming on buckets, blowing trombones and trumpets. Police in riot gear stood menacingly in front of the hotel, then eventually dispersed the protest by force around 10 p.m. Related From Slate Shirin Ali The Frightening, Very Real Tool ICE Agents Have to Add You to a "Nice Little Database" if You Attend a Protest Read More Popular in News & Politics Trump's Plan for an American Gulag Just Got a Major Boost in Court "I'm not as afraid as I thought I would be," a woman who goes by L told me at the Hilton Wide-Awake. She wore a backpack with a piece of paper taped to the back that said "Free Supplies": first aid, handwarmers, earplugs. "I guess I just feel like I can take it, so I want to put myself as a body here. They can't hurt all of us if it's really big, and there are a lot of people who can't necessarily be out here or don't want to take the risk. But because I feel like I can accept that risk, I have to." In three days, I will drive out of Minneapolis and back to my home in New York City. Tim, and Star, and the man with the beard, and the woman who did not want to answer—these people are already home. No one asked them if they wanted to be on the front lines of the fight against ICE. They did not volunteer for this assignment. The assignment came to them. "I wish I had the answer for why are we doing it," Star says. "It's an act of service. I don't know. And it's—so you can't save everybody, right? And you maybe can't even save anybody. Like maybe all we do is frustrate. You know, give someone one more day with their daughter or their dad. But if you lost your dad, or you lost your daughter, you would give anything to have one more day with them. And that's what we can do." Get the best of news and politics Sign up for Slate's evening newsletter. Thanks for signing up! You can manage your newsletter subscriptions at any time. You're already subscribed to the The Slatest newsletter. You can manage your newsletter subscriptions at any time. We encountered an issue signing you up.
---

```json
{
  "lean": "Left",
  "confidence": 0.92,
  "reasoning": "This article demonstrates strong left-leaning editorial choices through its deeply sympathetic framing of anti-ICE activism, portrayal of ICE as a threatening force ('secret police,' 'bastards'), and heroic depiction of protesters. The narrative structure presents activists as courageous community defenders while characterizing immigration enforcement as dangerous and illegitimate. The article lacks any perspective from ICE, law enforcement, or those supporting immigration enforcement, instead providing an immersive, first-person account that fully embraces the protesters' worldview. The language choices ('abduct them, disappear them'), comparisons to historical injustices, and uncritical acceptance of activist narratives all signal strong progressive political alignment.",
  "key_indicators": [
    "Sympathetic portrayal of anti-ICE activists as heroic community defenders",
    "ICE characterized with hostile language: 'secret police,' 'bastards,' agents who have 'killed'",
    "No perspectives from immigration enforcement or those supporting enforcement",
    "Explicit editorial choices like joining protesters (taking the whistle) and questioning journalistic ethics",
    "Comparison of ICE enforcement to sharecropping/historical oppression",
    "First-person immersive narrative that fully adopts activist perspective",
    "Framing of immigration enforcement as illegitimate threat to community safety"
  ]
}
```
